16. Information Measures for Continuous Variables

We previously developed entropy for random variables over finite alphabets . Now we will extend to infinite alphabets. Most notably, differential entropy is not as nice, but mutual information and information divergence remain nice.

16.1. Uncountably Infinite Alphabets

We focus on continuous variables for which there exists a PDF.

16.2. Inference and Modeling

Recall the capacity-redundancy theorem (Theorem 15.2) was used in the optimization framework of lecture 15 to turn the minimax problem into a maximin problem. The following fairly general theorem is an extension:

Theorem 16.1 (Sion's Minimax Theorem). Let be a compact convex subset, let be a convex set, and let satisfy:

  1. is continuous and concave on for each ; and
  2. is continuous and convex on for each .

Then

(16.8)

TO apply this theorem, we choose , which for finite is a compact set, and choose which is a convex set. Then define

(16.9)

And then we get mixture models of the form

(16.10)

and the model capacity is analogously,

(16.11)

Model capacity is unaffected by reparameterization. When is a continuous alphabet, there is a less powerful extension but we omit the details.

16.3.2. Conditional Entropy

16.3.4. Information divergence

For Gaussians that differ only in their means:

The divergence takes the form of a scaled signal to noise ratio (for a likelihood test determining whether an observation comes from or ) where the signal is and the noise is

16.4. Approximating Distributions by Exponential Families*

There is a sequence of exponential families such that any sufficiently well-behaved distribution over a continuous alphabet is arbitrarily close to a distribution in this sequence of families. The measure of closeness we use is information divergence.

Proof uses Stone-Weierstrass. I.e. take polynomial approximation to and that take exponential of it.
